serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cookie with sprinkles weighs approximately 30 g and provides around 123 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Cookie with sprinkles — Singapore HPB Foodo Database (F11080019)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo record F11080019 for Cookie with sprinkles. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken verbatim from this record. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entry 18158 (Cookies, sugar, commercially prepared) as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown for this record; this adaptation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Cookies — University of Sydney GI Database (analogous: sugar cookie / plain sweet biscuit, GI ~55)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for 'cookie with sprinkles'. GI of 55 is adopted from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain sugar cookies / sweet biscuits (GI ~55). Per the standard GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70), a GI of exactly 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category. Glycemic load per 30 g serving calculated as: (55 × 78.31 × 0.30) / 100 = 12.9, rounded to 13.0. The high sugar content (45.78 g/100 g) and zero dietary fibre are consistent with this GI profile.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sugar (45.78 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and promotes weight gain, both of which independently elevate blood pressure.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid cookies with sprinkles; the combination of 45.78 g/100 g sugar, zero fibre, and 229 mg/100 g sodium promotes fluid retention and cardiovascular strain.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate load of 78.31 g/100 g with sugar at 45.78 g/100 g and zero dietary fibre produces rapid and severe postprandial blood glucose elevation, far exceeding safe thresholds for glycaemic management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Persons with Type 2 Diabetes must avoid this food; the 78.31 g/100 g carbohydrate with 45.78 g/100 g sugar and no fibre will cause acute hyperglycaemia even in small portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat (~3.5 g/100 g), trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g), and very high refined sugar (45.78 g/100 g) collectively promote dyslipidaemia, endothelial inflammation, and atherogenic risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid cookies with sprinkles due to the combined atherogenic burden of saturated fat, trans fat, and extremely high refined sugar content.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Extremely high refined sugar (45.78 g/100 g) and estimated trans fat promote systemic inflammation, worsening RA disease activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Persons with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this food; the high refined sugar and trans fat content strongly promote pro-inflammatory mediators that exacerbate joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cookie with sprinkles is a sweet baked confection made from a standard sugar cookie dough — typically comprising wheat flour, butter, sugar, eggs, and vanilla — decorated with colourful sugar sprinkles (also called hundreds-and-thousands or nonpareils) pressed onto the surface before or after baking. The texture is crisp to slightly chewy depending on baking time, with a buttery, sweet flavour and a visually festive appearance due to the multicoloured sugar decorations. This product is classified as ultra-processed and is widely consumed as a snack or treat across international markets, including Singapore. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense at 410 kcal/100 g, with an extremely high sugar content of 45.78 g/100 g and a total carbohydrate load of 78.31 g/100 g, while providing negligible dietary fibre (0.0 g/100 g). Fat is 7.83 g/100 g with an estimated saturated fat fraction of approximately 3.5 g/100 g and trace trans fat from butter. Sodium is 229 mg/100 g. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low, at the upper boundary of the low GI range) based on analogous sugar cookie data from the University of Sydney GI Database, but the glycemic load per 30 g serving is approximately 13, and the zero-fibre profile means glucose absorption is unmodulated. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high refined sugar, saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium content. It should be consumed only occasionally and in small portions by healthy individuals.
common_user_queries: Can a diabetic eat sprinkle cookies?
How many calories in a cookie with sprinkles?
Is sprinkle cookie suitable for hypertension?
What is the sugar content of a decorated cookie?
Can I eat cookies with sprinkles if I have heart disease?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo record F11080019 with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entry 18158 (Cookies, sugar, commercially prepared), which is the closest approved-source equivalent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain sugar cookies. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo F11080019; this is a refined flour and sugar product with no fibre-containing ingredients. 2. calcium_mg is 0.0 and iron_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo F11080019 for this product formulation. 3. potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data not reported in HPB Foodo F11080019 for this specific product; USDA FoodData Central entry 18158 provides partial micronutrient data but was not used for these fields to avoid mixing source granularity. 4. Saturated fat (3.5 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (2.8 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.9 g/100 g), and trans_fat_g (0.1 g/100 g) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entry 18158 (Cookies, sugar, commercially prepared) as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown for this record. 5. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain sugar cookies; no direct GI measurement exists for 'cookie with sprinkles'. Per the standard GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), a GI of 55 is classified as 'low'. The gi_category has been corrected from 'medium' to 'low' to align with this classification boundary. 6. Halal status assumed based on standard ingredient profile; individual product certification should be verified. 7. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected gi_category from an earlier inconsistency; validator confirmed gi_category 'medium' did not match glycemic_index 55.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — GI value of 55 sits at the upper boundary of the low GI category per the standard classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Updated gi_source notes and natural_language_description to reflect this correction. Updated notes field to document the correction rationale.
